content.full_text:
(916) 322·5662 
Michael A. Bojanowski 
Planning Commissioner 
17477 Ter Holland Lane 
Healdsburg, CA 
94558 
Dear Mr. Bojanowski: 
Administration 
Executive/legal 
322·5901 
Enforcement 
322-6441 
Statements of Economic Interest 
322-64"" 
322·5660 
March 5, 1985 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-85-019 
Judy Carlson of our staff referred to me your question 
regarding reporting obligations on a Statement of Economic 
Interests. 
You are a developer, and you own more than 10 percent of a 
development company. 
You have asked whether you must disclose 
the names of individual buyers on Schedule H-l. 
If an official owns a 10 percent or greater interest in a 
business entity, the official must disclose an individual or 
entity's name if the official's pro rata share of the payments 
from that individual or entity was $10,000 or more during the 
reporting period. 
Government Code Sections 82030 and 
87207(b) (2). 
Thus, you would report the name of each individual 
buyer if the payment met the threshold described above. 
You also asked whether the answer would be the same if the 
project were very large, for example, if it were over 100 
homes? 
You would still report the names of the individual 
buyers. 
If you are not directly involved with the sales 
transactions on the homes, you may not be aware of each of the 
individual purchasers; the Political Reform Act requires you to 
disclose those buyers of whom you know or have reason to know. 
It is my understanding that you may need to amend 
Schedule H-l of your 1983 Annual Statement of Economic 
Interests. 
The enclosed supplemental forms are provided for 
that purpose. 
The completed supplemental forms should be filed 


Michael A. Bojanowski 
March 5, 1985 
Page 2 
with your clerk who will retain a copy and forward the originals 
to the Fair Political Practices Commission. 
I hope this has clarified the disclosure provisions for 
you. 
If I can be of additional help please feel free to contact 
me at (916) 322-5662. 
JET:plh 
Enclosures 
Sincerely, 
Jeanette E. Turvill 
Legal Assistant 


State of California 
Memorandum 
To 
: Barbara 
Date 
January 31, 1985 
From 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
Judy C. 
Subject: Statements of Economic Interests 
I spoke today with a County Planning Commissioner, 
Michael A. Bojanowski, regarding filing a Supplemental 
Schedule H to his 1983 Annual Statement of Economic Interests. 
During our conversation he asked me the following question: 
If a developer builds and sells a large number of homes 
and the pro-rata share of each sale, either to himself as 
a sole owner, or to a business entity in which he has a 10% 
or greater interest, is $10,000 or more, does he have to 
report the name of each individual buyer on Schedule H-l? 
What would happen if the project was very large, say over 100 
homes? 
You can write to Mr. Bojanowski at: 
17577 Ter Holland Lane 
Healdsburg, CA 94558 
Thank you.

sections.question: If a developer builds and sells a large number of homes and the pro-rata share of each sale, either to himself as a sole owner, or to a business entity in which he has a 10% or greater interest, is $10,000 or more, does he have to report the name of each individual buyer on Schedule H-1? What would happen if the project was very large, say over 100 homes?
sections.conclusion: If an official owns a 10 percent or greater interest in a business entity, the official must disclose an individual or entity's name if the official's pro rata share of the payments from that individual or entity was $10,000 or more during the reporting period. Government Code Sections 82030 and 87207(b)(2). Thus, you would report the name of each individual buyer if the payment met the threshold described above. You would still report the names of the individual buyers even if the project is very large. If you are not directly involved with the sales transactions on the homes, you may not be aware of each of the individual purchasers; the Political Reform Act requires you to disclose those buyers of whom you know or have reason to know.
sections.question_synthetic: Must a planning commissioner who is a developer owning 10% or more of a development company disclose the names of individual home buyers on Schedule H-1 of their Statement of Economic Interests, and does the answer change if the project involves over 100 homes?
sections.conclusion_synthetic: Yes, a developer must disclose individual buyer names on Schedule H-1 if the pro rata share of payments from each buyer is $10,000 or more, regardless of project size. The disclosure obligation applies to all buyers of whom the official knows or has reason to know, even if not directly involved in individual transactions.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains both an internal referral memo from January 31, 1985 and the formal FPPC response letter dated March 5, 1985. Clear question and conclusion sections present. Minor address discrepancy noted (17477 vs 17577 Ter Holland Lane) in source documents but does not affect legal analysis extraction.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: If a developer builds and sells a large number of homes and the pro-rata share of each sale, either to himself as a sole owner, or to a business entity in which he has a 10% or greater interest, is $10,000 or more, does he have to report the name of each individual buyer on Schedule H-1? What would happen if the project was very large, say over 100 homes?

CONCLUSION: If an official owns a 10 percent or greater interest in a business entity, the official must disclose an individual or entity's name if the official's pro rata share of the payments from that individual or entity was $10,000 or more during the reporting period. Government Code Sections 82030 and 87207(b)(2). Thus, you would report the name of each individual buyer if the payment met the threshold described above. You would still report the names of the individual buyers even if the project is very large. If you are not directly involved with the sales transactions on the homes, you may not be aware of each of the individual purchasers; the Political Reform Act requires you to disclose those buyers of whom you know or have reason to know.

Subject: Statements of Economic Interests I spoke today with a County Planning Commissioner, Michael A. Bojanowski, regarding filing a Supplemental Schedule H to his 1983 Annual Statement of Economic Interests. During our conversation he asked me the following question: If a developer builds and sells a large number of homes and the pro-rata share of each sale, either to himself as a sole owner, or to a business entity in which he has a 10% or greater interest, is $10,000 or more, does he have to report the name of each individual buyer on Schedule H-l? embedding.summary: FPPC advice letter responding to a planning commissioner/developer's question about disclosure obligations for individual home buyer names on Schedule H-1 of Economic Interest statements. The letter clarifies that buyers must be disclosed if the official's pro rata share of payments meets the $10,000 threshold, regardless of project size.
